mission.title: Install and Configure Falcosidekick (Falco) on Kubernetes
mission.description: Falcosidekick is a daemon that connects Falco to your ecosystem, forwarding Falco events to various outputs. Installing Falcosidekick allows you to enhance your security monitoring by integrating Falco alerts with your existing tools and workflows.

steps.title: Add the Falcosidekick Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official Falcosidekick Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration with the following command:
```bash
helm repo add falcosidekick https://falcosecurity.github.io/charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Falcosidekick using Helm
steps.description:
Install Falcosidekick in your Kubernetes cluster using the following command. This command creates a namespace called 'falcosidekick' if it doesn't exist:
```bash
helm install falcosidekick falcosidekick/falcosidekick --namespace falcosidekick --create-namespace --version 2.32.0
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the Falcosidekick pod is running by executing:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace falcosidekick
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
To ensure optimal performance, configure resource limits for the Falcosidekick deployment. Edit the deployment with:
```bash
kubectl edit deployment falcosidekick --namespace falcosidekick
```
Then, add the following resource limits under the containers section:
```yaml
resources:
  limits:
    memory: "256Mi"
    cpu: "500m"
  requests:
    memory: "128Mi"
    cpu: "250m"
```

steps.title: Set up port forwarding for access
steps.description:
To access the Falcosidekick service, set up port forwarding with the following command:
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/falcosidekick --namespace falcosidekick 2801:2801
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Falcosidekick will show the pod running in the 'falcosidekick' namespace, and you will be able to access the service via port forwarding. Additionally, resource limits will be configured to ensure efficient operation.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add falcosidekick https://falcosecurity.github.io/charts
helm repo update

resources:
  limits:
    memory: "256Mi"
    cpu: "500m"
  requests:
    memory: "128Mi"
    cpu: "250m"

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Falcosidekick Helm release from your Kubernetes cluster with the following command. This will remove the deployment and associated resources:
```bash
helm uninstall falcosidekick --namespace falcosidekick
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Remove any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent resources that were created during the installation. Use the following commands:
```bash
kubectl delete crd falcosidekick.falcosecurity.io
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace falcosidekick
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the 'falcosidekick' namespace to clean up any remaining resources. Verify that the namespace has been removed:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace falcosidekick
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, it's important to back up the current configuration and state of Falcosidekick. You can do this by exporting the current configuration:
```bash
kubectl get all --namespace falcosidekick -o yaml > falcosidekick-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and upgrade the Falcosidekick release to the latest version. Run the following commands:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade falcosidekick falcosidekick/falcosidekick --namespace falcosidekick --version 2.33.0
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check that the Falcosidekick pod is running the new version after the upgrade. Use the following command to verify:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace falcosidekick
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If the Falcosidekick pod is in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for errors:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace falcosidekick
```
Common issues include misconfigured environment variables or resource limits.

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If you cannot access the Falcosidekick service, ensure that the port forwarding is set up correctly. Check the service configuration:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace falcosidekick
```
If the service is not running, restart it with:
```bash
kubectl delete svc falcosidekick --namespace falcosidekick
helm install falcosidekick falcosidekick/falcosidekick --namespace falcosidekick
```

troubleshooting.title: High memory usage
troubleshooting.description:
If you notice high memory usage, check the resource limits set for the Falcosidekick deployment. You can edit the deployment with:
```bash
kubectl edit deployment falcosidekick --namespace falcosidekick
```
Adjust the resource limits as necessary.

troubleshooting.title: Failed to connect to Falco
troubleshooting.description:
If Falcosidekick fails to connect to Falco, verify that the Falco service is running and reachable. Check the Falco pod status:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace falco
```
If it's not running, troubleshoot the Falco installation.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with Helm and kubectl installed.
